## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Operator-driven manual walkthrough from a marshal-zeroed state, positioned between canonical-establish (built) and canonical-onboarding-sequence (yet to be built). Two purposes under the gauntlet design:
  
  1. Validate canonical-establish in actually-fresh marshal-zero state — its precondition probes were designed defensively, but only running against a real fresh state surfaces silent first-build assumptions in the underlying tabtargets.
  2. Inform canonical-onboarding-sequence case design — walking §3 manually surfaces the case shape, probe specifications, and operator-facing diagnostic ceremony for the next fixture's cases.
  
  Cognitive posture: real-time conversation, surprise-by-surprise. Operator drives commands; agent watches output, reads code on demand, captures dispositions.
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  §2 (canonical-establish run as a fixture against marshal-zero), §3 (reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain, walked manually), and §4 (crucible suite per RELEASE.md) each completed end-to-end against a fresh marshal-zero. Every surprise captured + dispositioned (inline fix or surfaced as follow-up pace before canonical-onboarding-sequence mounts). Fixed-or-surfaced is sufficient — green tally is BBAAX's guardrail, not this pace's.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt. Same magnitude as BBAAX, distinct purpose: this pace's bug surface is decomposed step-by-step (manual operator drive) and informs probe design before the next fixture exists; BBAAX validates the fully-stitched suite end-to-end.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract.
  - Per BBAAN posture: invest is fatal-on-exists; divest deletes production RBRA; unmake absorbs governor cleanup. Any retry path goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery.
  - Reuse existing tabtargets exclusively; canonical-establish runs as a fixture; §3 and §4 walked manually.
  - No code changes to canonical-establish during this pace (it's already built). Surprises in canonical-establish surface as follow-up paces.
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-canonical-establish landed (the §2 fixture exists for run-as-fixture step).
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - §1 (pristine-lifecycle exercises it; not relevant here).
  - Canonical-onboarding-sequence implementation (subsequent pace, informed by this walkthrough's findings).
  - Suite assembly (subsequent pace).
  - Green-tally end-to-end run (BBAAX).
  - Fixing every surprise inline — large or design-call surprises surface as follow-up paces.

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
75	
76	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
77	
78	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
79	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
80	
81	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
82	
83	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
84	
85	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
86	
87	## Suite-level fail-fast
88	
89	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
90	
91	## No-teardown decision
92	
93	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
94	
95	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
96	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
97	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
98	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
99	
100	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
101	
102	## Operator scope
103	
104	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.
105	
106	## Coupling to ₣A_
107	
108	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
109	
110	## Locked design decisions — pointers
111	
112	- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
113	- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
114	
115	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
116	
117	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
118	
119	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
120	
121	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
122	
123	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
124	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
125	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
126	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
127	
128	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
129	
130	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
131	
132	## References
133	
134	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
135	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
136	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
137	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
138	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
139	
140	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAc
141	
142	## Character
143	
144	Operator-driven manual walkthrough from a marshal-zeroed state, positioned between canonical-establish (built) and canonical-onboarding-sequence (yet to be built). Two purposes under the gauntlet design:
145	
146	1. Validate canonical-establish in actually-fresh marshal-zero state — its precondition probes were designed defensively, but only running against a real fresh state surfaces silent first-build assumptions in the underlying tabtargets.
147	2. Inform canonical-onboarding-sequence case design — walking §3 manually surfaces the case shape, probe specifications, and operator-facing diagnostic ceremony for the next fixture's cases.
148	
149	Cognitive posture: real-time conversation, surprise-by-surprise. Operator drives commands; agent watches output, reads code on demand, captures dispositions.
150	
151	## Wrap criterion
152	
153	§2 (canonical-establish run as a fixture against marshal-zero), §3 (reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain, walked manually), and §4 (crucible suite per RELEASE.md) each completed end-to-end against a fresh marshal-zero. Every surprise captured + dispositioned (inline fix or surfaced as follow-up pace before canonical-onboarding-sequence mounts). Fixed-or-surfaced is sufficient — green tally is BBAAX's guardrail, not this pace's.
154	
155	## Cost
156	
157	~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt. Same magnitude as BBAAX, distinct purpose: this pace's bug surface is decomposed step-by-step (manual operator drive) and informs probe design before the next fixture exists; BBAAX validates the fully-stitched suite end-to-end.
158	
159	## Constraints
160	
161	- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract.
162	- Per BBAAN posture: invest is fatal-on-exists; divest deletes production RBRA; unmake absorbs governor cleanup. Any retry path goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery.
163	- Reuse existing tabtargets exclusively; canonical-establish runs as a fixture; §3 and §4 walked manually.
164	- No code changes to canonical-establish during this pace (it's already built). Surprises in canonical-establish surface as follow-up paces.
165	
166	## Cross-pace dependencies
167	
168	- gauntlet-canonical-establish landed (the §2 fixture exists for run-as-fixture step).
169	
170	## Out of scope
171	
172	- §1 (pristine-lifecycle exercises it; not relevant here).
173	- Canonical-onboarding-sequence implementation (subsequent pace, informed by this walkthrough's findings).
174	- Suite assembly (subsequent pace).
175	- Green-tally end-to-end run (BBAAX).
176	- Fixing every surprise inline — large or design-call surprises surface as follow-up paces.

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions — pointers

- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAc

## Character

Operator-driven manual walkthrough from a marshal-zeroed state, positioned between canonical-establish (built) and canonical-onboarding-sequence (yet to be built). Two purposes under the gauntlet design:

1. Validate canonical-establish in actually-fresh marshal-zero state — its precondition probes were designed defensively, but only running against a real fresh state surfaces silent first-build assumptions in the underlying tabtargets.
2. Inform canonical-onboarding-sequence case design — walking §3 manually surfaces the case shape, probe specifications, and operator-facing diagnostic ceremony for the next fixture's cases.

Cognitive posture: real-time conversation, surprise-by-surprise. Operator drives commands; agent watches output, reads code on demand, captures dispositions.

## Wrap criterion

§2 (canonical-establish run as a fixture against marshal-zero), §3 (reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain, walked manually), and §4 (crucible suite per RELEASE.md) each completed end-to-end against a fresh marshal-zero. Every surprise captured + dispositioned (inline fix or surfaced as follow-up pace before canonical-onboarding-sequence mounts). Fixed-or-surfaced is sufficient — green tally is BBAAX's guardrail, not this pace's.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt. Same magnitude as BBAAX, distinct purpose: this pace's bug surface is decomposed step-by-step (manual operator drive) and informs probe design before the next fixture exists; BBAAX validates the fully-stitched suite end-to-end.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract.
- Per BBAAN posture: invest is fatal-on-exists; divest deletes production RBRA; unmake absorbs governor cleanup. Any retry path goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery.
- Reuse existing tabtargets exclusively; canonical-establish runs as a fixture; §3 and §4 walked manually.
- No code changes to canonical-establish during this pace (it's already built). Surprises in canonical-establish surface as follow-up paces.

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-canonical-establish landed (the §2 fixture exists for run-as-fixture step).

## Out of scope

- §1 (pristine-lifecycle exercises it; not relevant here).
- Canonical-onboarding-sequence implementation (subsequent pace, informed by this walkthrough's findings).
- Suite assembly (subsequent pace).
- Green-tally end-to-end run (BBAAX).
- Fixing every surprise inline — large or design-call surprises surface as follow-up paces.

285	######################################################################
286	# External Functions (rbgv_*)
287	
288	# Probe 1: JWT SA Access Probe
289	#
290	# For each iteration:
291	#   1. Exchange JWT for OAuth token via rbgo_get_token_capture with role's RBRA file
292	#   2. Call Artifact Registry packages.list to verify token grants read access
293	#   3. Sleep for configured delay
294	#
295	# All three roles have read access:
296	#   Governor  = Owner
297	#   Director  = repoAdmin
298	#   Retriever = reader
299	rbgv_jwt_sa_probe() {
300	  zrbgv_sentinel
301	
302	  local z_role="${1:-}"
303	  local z_count="${2:-1}"
304	  local z_delay_ms="${3:-0}"
305	
306	  buc_doc_brief "Probe JWT service account access for a given role against Artifact Registry"
307	  buc_doc_param "role"     "Role to probe: governor | director | retriever"
308	  buc_doc_param "count"    "Number of iterations (default: 1)"
309	  buc_doc_param "delay_ms" "Milliseconds to sleep between iterations (default: 0)"
310	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
311	
312	  test -n "${z_role}"  || buc_die "role parameter required (governor | director | retriever)"
313	  test -n "${z_count}" || buc_die "count parameter required"
314	
315	  buc_step "JWT SA access probe: role=${z_role} count=${z_count} delay_ms=${z_delay_ms}"
316	
317	  local z_iter=1
318	  while test "${z_iter}" -le "${z_count}"; do
319	    buc_step "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iter}/${z_count}"
320	
321	    zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once "${z_role}" "${z_iter}"
322	
323	    if test "${z_iter}" -lt "${z_count}" && test "${z_delay_ms}" -gt 0; then
324	      local z_sleep
325	      z_sleep=$(zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture "${z_delay_ms}")

327	      sleep "${z_sleep}"
328	    fi

done
}

# Execute one Artifact Registry packages.list probe iteration for a JWT SA role.
# Writes response to ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE, HTTP code to ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE,
# and stderr to ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE (kindle-constant paths for forensic visibility).
zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_role="${1}"
  local z_iteration="${2}"

  buc_log_args "JWT SA probe iteration ${z_iteration} for role '${z_role}'"

  buc_log_args "Resolve RBRA file for role"
  local z_rbra_file
  z_rbra_file=$(zrbgv_role_rbra_file_capture "${z_role}") || return 1

  buc_log_args "Validate RBRA file exists"
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "RBRA file not found for role ${z_role}: ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "Exchange JWT for OAuth token"
  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to obtain OAuth token for role ${z_role} (iteration ${z_iteration})"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Empty OAuth token for role ${z_role} (iteration ${z_iteration})"

  buc_log_args "Build Artifact Registry packages.list URL"
  test -n "${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID is not set"
  test -n "${RBRR_GCP_REGION:-}"       || buc_die "RBRR_GCP_REGION is not set"
  test -n "${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY:-}"   || buc_die "RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY is not set"

  local -r z_ar_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY}${RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1}/projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${RBRR_GCP_REGION}/repositories/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/packages"
  local -r z_ar_label="JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}"

  buc_log_args "Call Artifact Registry packages.list (with transient-5xx retry)"
  zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry \
    "${z_ar_label}"             \
    "${z_ar_url}"               \
    "${z_token}"                \
    "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}"     \
    "${ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE}"     \
    "${ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE}"

  local z_code
  z_code=$(<"${ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE}") || buc_die "Failed to read AR HTTP code file"
  test -n "${z_code}"                || buc_die "Empty HTTP code from AR curl"

  buc_log_args "AR packages.list HTTP ${z_code} for role ${z_role} iteration ${z_iteration}"

  case "${z_code}" in
    200|206)
      buc_step "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: OK (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    401|403)
      buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: access denied (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    *)
      local z_err=""
      if jq -e . "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null) || z_err="Unknown error"
      else
        z_err="Non-JSON response (HTTP ${z_code})"
      fi
      buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
      ;;
  esac
}

# Execute one CRM projects.get probe iteration for the Payor OAuth flow.
# Writes response to ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE, HTTP code to ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE,
# and stderr to ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE (kindle-constant paths for forensic visibility).
zrbgv_payor_crm_probe_once() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_iteration="${1}"

  buc_log_args "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}"

  buc_log_args "Authenticate via Payor OAuth refresh token flow"
  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) \
    || buc_die "Failed to obtain Payor OAuth access token (iteration ${z_iteration})"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Empty Payor OAuth access token (iteration ${z_iteration})"

  buc_log_args "Build CRM projects.get URL"
  test -n "${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID is not set"

  local -r z_crm_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V1}/projects/${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}"
  local -r z_crm_label="Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}"

  buc_log_args "Call CRM projects.get on payor project (with transient-5xx retry)"
  zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry \
    "${z_crm_label}"            \
    "${z_crm_url}"              \
    "${z_token}"                \
    "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}"    \
    "${ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE}"    \
    "${ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE}"

  local z_code
  z_code=$(<"${ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE}") || buc_die "Failed to read CRM HTTP code file"
  test -n "${z_code}"                 || buc_die "Empty HTTP code from CRM curl"

  buc_log_args "CRM projects.get HTTP ${z_code} for Payor iteration ${z_iteration}"

  case "${z_code}" in
    200)
      buc_step "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: OK (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    401|403)
      buc_die "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: access denied (HTTP ${z_code})"
      ;;
    *)
      local z_err=""
      if jq -e . "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null) || z_err="Unknown error"
      else
        z_err="Non-JSON response (HTTP ${z_code})"
      fi
      buc_die "Payor OAuth probe iteration ${z_iteration}: unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
      ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbgv_*)

# Probe 1: JWT SA Access Probe
#
# For each iteration:
#   1. Exchange JWT for OAuth token via rbgo_get_token_capture with role's RBRA file
#   2. Call Artifact Registry packages.list to verify token grants read access
#   3. Sleep for configured delay
#
# All three roles have read access:
#   Governor  = Owner
#   Director  = repoAdmin
#   Retriever = reader
rbgv_jwt_sa_probe() {
  zrbgv_sentinel

  local z_role="${1:-}"
  local z_count="${2:-1}"
  local z_delay_ms="${3:-0}"

  buc_doc_brief "Probe JWT service account access for a given role against Artifact Registry"
  buc_doc_param "role"     "Role to probe: governor | director | retriever"
  buc_doc_param "count"    "Number of iterations (default: 1)"
  buc_doc_param "delay_ms" "Milliseconds to sleep between iterations (default: 0)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_role}"  || buc_die "role parameter required (governor | director | retriever)"
  test -n "${z_count}" || buc_die "count parameter required"

  buc_step "JWT SA access probe: role=${z_role} count=${z_count} delay_ms=${z_delay_ms}"

  local z_iter=1
  while test "${z_iter}" -le "${z_count}"; do
    buc_step "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iter}/${z_count}"

    zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once "${z_role}" "${z_iter}"

    if test "${z_iter}" -lt "${z_count}" && test "${z_delay_ms}" -gt 0; then
      local z_sleep
      z_sleep=$(zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture "${z_delay_ms}")
      buc_log_args "Sleeping ${z_sleep}s (${z_delay_ms}ms) before next iteration"
      sleep "${z_sleep}"
    fi

content.text:
## Diagnosis: IAM-binding propagation race in retriever_invest

**Failure point:** `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` case → access-probe step → HTTP 403 on iteration 1.

**The invest itself succeeded:**
- SA `retriever-canest-ret@canc-d-canest2100002` created (uid 115780635167081740940)
- Key generated (BBAAp's consumer-side retry absorbed the JWT-propagation race at attempt 1 — that's working)
- Both IAM roles granted via REST: `artifactregistry.reader` + `containeranalysis.occurrences.viewer`
- RBRA written to assay slot per BBAAo

**Verified via gcloud (~5 min after failure):**
- IAM policy on `canc-d-canest2100002` **now** shows both bindings for the retriever SA.
- GAR repo `canc-canest2100002-gar` exists at the exact URL the probe hits (created 11:04:15, ~33s before probe).

**Structural cause — `Tools/rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh:214–216`:**
```
401|403)
  buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: access denied (HTTP ${z_code})"
```

The probe `buc_die`s on the **first** 401/403 — no propagation absorption. The "count=5 delay_ms=1500" loop (rbgv:317–328) is for **stability sampling** (5 successful reads in a row), not initial-failure retry. The 5xx retry path exists (`zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry`), but 4xx is treated terminal.

**Why this race is different from BBAAp's:**
- BBAAp absorbs the **OAuth-token-mint** race (`invalid_grant` + `Invalid JWT Signature` — SA-key freshly minted, not yet readable by Google's OAuth backend).
- This is the **IAM-policy-read** race (SetIamPolicy returned success, but downstream resource API hasn't seen the grant yet — eventually consistent across GCP's policy backplane, typical 7–60s, occasionally longer).
- The retriever's invest finished and probe fired ~14s later — squarely in the typical race window. BBAAp doesn't reach this layer; its retry is at token mint, not resource access.

**This is a clean gauntlet-walkthrough surprise** — exactly the silent-first-build assumption the pace exists to catch. The probe was written for *post-stable-state* verification; under fresh-MZ first-mint, the propagation window is a real failure mode that no prior tier (`rbw-tf`, `rbw-tr`) exercises against fresh state.

**Disposition options when we get to repair** (deferred per your instruction):
- **(A)** Add bounded 401/403 retry inside `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once` with a propagation budget. Aligns with BBAAp's consumer-side-absorption posture.
- **(B)** Producer-side wait inside `rbgg_invest_retriever`: post-grant probe loop until 200, before declaring invest complete. Localizes to the producer that knows the grant just landed.
- **(C)** Hybrid: split the probe loop into "absorb propagation" prelude (tolerates 403 with budget) + "stability sample" main (current strict behavior).

Want me to surface this as a follow-up pace now, or hold and continue the walkthrough first?

message.content: Help me think through a clean choice here.  I really want to avoid lots of forks, avoid creating a thicket of if/then/else permutations.  What is a simple way to proceed here?  I'm thinking more retries at other levels, but only for signatures we recognize as 'not yet'.

content.text:
## Thinking it through

Two structural facts shape the choice:

1. **The 403 body shape is ambiguous.** Unlike BBAAp's `invalid_grant + "Invalid JWT Signature."` (a Google-OAuth signature that means *only* propagation), a GCP 403 from Artifact Registry is identical for "policy still propagating" and "you genuinely lack the role." We can't discriminate by body — we'd have to discriminate by *lifecycle position* ("we just granted; this is the propagation window").
2. **Lifecycle position lives at the producer**, not the consumer. The invest function knows it just granted the role; the probe doesn't. That's why BBAAp's exact pattern (consumer-side absorption inside `rbgo_get_token_capture`) doesn't transplant cleanly here — it would force the probe to know "am I in a fresh-mint window or not?"

## The clean version — producer-side propagation wait

Single helper, one call locus per producer, no conditionals:

```
rbgg_await_iam_grant_propagation(role, rbra_file, budget_s)
  loop until budget exhausted:
    token = rbgo_get_token_capture(rbra_file)   # already absorbs the JWT race
    code  = HTTP GET on AR packages.list as that token
    case code in
      200) return success
      401|403) sleep+grow; continue
      *) return failure   # fail-fast on unexpected
```

Hook into `rbgg_invest_retriever` and `rbgg_invest_director` immediately after the role-grant block, before install-advice. Governor mantle: already self-validates elsewhere; revisit only if needed.

**Probe stays strict** — `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` unchanged. Probe's job is "verify post-stable state"; that contract is preserved. No semantic conflation between *absorb propagation* and *stability sample*.

## Why this matches your "no thicket" criterion

- **One new helper, two call sites.** No conditional branching anywhere.
- **Each function does one thing.** Producer says "I am done when grant is observably effective." Probe says "current state is/isn't accessible." Errors are unambiguous: budget-exhausted at producer ≠ access-denied at probe.
- **Mirrors BBAAp's principle, not its mechanics.** BBAAp's principle: *absorb the race at the boundary where it's structurally recognized.* For OAuth-mint that boundary is consumer-side (every consumer hits it). For IAM-propagation that boundary is producer-side (only the granter knows the window opened).
- **Reuses BBAAp.** The producer wait calls `rbgo_get_token_capture`, which already absorbs the JWT-propagation race. So this is the "next layer up" of the same retry stack — exactly the "more retries at other levels for signatures we recognize as 'not yet'" you described. Each layer recognizes its own 'not yet' and retries; nothing else has to know.

## What I'd surface as a follow-up pace

A pace named something like `iam-grant-propagation-wait` with this shape, defined to be hooked into invest_retriever and invest_director and (lightly) verified against governor mantle. Out-of-scope guard: probe code untouched.

Want me to draft that pace docket, or would you rather keep walking first and capture the full set of surprises before slating?

content.text:
Got it. The body confirms it's exactly the standard GCP IAM-denied response — `IAM_PERMISSION_DENIED` shape, identical for both race and real denial. Body shape isn't the discriminator. **The discriminator is the bounded budget itself**, same as BBAAp.

## The clean version — consumer-side absorption inside the probe

Single change. In `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once` (rbgv_AccessProbe.sh:166–227), the case branch becomes:

```
case "${z_code}" in
  200|206)  ok                                      # current
  401|403)  if budget remaining: sleep+grow, retry  # NEW
            else:                buc_die             # current message
  *)        buc_die                                  # current
esac
```

- **Budget** lives on the probe-once call (e.g., 120s — IAM propagation can run 60–120s, occasionally longer; 2s initial, 2x growth, 15s cap, mirroring BBAAp's shape).
- **Each retry re-mints the token** via `rbgo_get_token_capture` (already absorbs the JWT-mint race per BBAAp). So we get layered absorption for free: token-mint race at L1, IAM-propagation race at L2.
- **Past budget on 4xx** → `buc_die` exactly as today. Same operator-facing message, same exit shape.
- **Other 4xx** (404, 400, 422, etc.) → die immediately, no retry. Real bugs surface fast.

## Why this matches your "no thicket"

- **One function changes.** `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once`. No new helpers, no new call sites, no producer hooks.
- **The probe's outer loop (count=5, delay_ms=1500) keeps its semantic** — stability sampling. Iteration 1 absorbs propagation; iterations 2–5 should complete fast since access is now stable. Flap detection retained.
- **Budget IS the signature-recognition primitive.** The signature we recognize as 'not yet': *401/403 within bounded budget after a token mint.* Past budget → not 'not yet'; it's a real denial.
- **Stack composes cleanly with BBAAp.** Layer 1 (`rbgo_get_token_capture`): JWT-signature race → retry. Layer 2 (`zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once`): IAM-propagation race → retry. Each layer recognizes its own 'not yet'. No layer has to know what the other does.
- **Mirrors BBAAp's posture exactly.** BBAAp moved producer-side gating into consumer-side retry inside the function every consumer calls; this does the same thing one layer up — every JWT-SA AR caller (today: just the probe; tomorrow: any future consumer using `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once`) absorbs uniformly.

## Scope sketch (deferring the actual write)

- Touched: `Tools/rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` only — `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once` plus a small constant block (RBGC budget constants paired with the BBAAp-family RBGC names).
- Untouched: probe outer loop, Payor CRM probe (different race, different domain), all producers (invest, mantle), test fixtures.
- Verification path: bash -n, theurge build, fast suite, then re-run the canonical-establish fixture from a fresh marshal-zero — same operator gesture you just ran.

content.text:
Yes — retained, but only barely. Two gaps worth fixing.

## Where the body lives now

`/var/folders/h1/.../T/rbtd-80541/burv/invoke-00005/temp/temp-20260428-110448-84816-287/rbgv_ar_resp.json`

That's macOS volatile temp:
- **Subject to OS cleanup** — macOS purges `/var/folders` periodically; on reboot or after a few days, gone.
- **Hidden three layers deep** — under `burv/invoke-NNNNN/temp/temp-XXX/`. No pointer in the case-level summary; you have to know the BURV layout and spelunk.
- **Not in the historical log** (`../logs-buk/`) — that log is durable, but the buc_die message only carries `(HTTP 403)`; the body bytes are absent.

## What's already there (and inconsistent)

`zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once` already has body-summary logic — but only on the catch-all branch:

```
*)   # unexpected HTTP code
  z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}")
  buc_die "... unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
```

The 401/403 branch above it doesn't extract anything — just `(HTTP 403)`. That's the inconsistency biting us. Had the 401/403 branch followed the catch-all's pattern, the historical log would have had `Permission 'artifactregistry.packages.list' denied on ... (or it may not exist).` — same body content I had to spelunk a temp path to find.

## Simple fix — rides the same pace

Add to the upcoming `iam-grant-propagation-retry` pace (or whatever we call it):

**Make 401/403's diagnostic mirror the catch-all's body-summary.** When the budget exhausts and `buc_die` fires, format:
```
JWT SA probe [retriever] iteration N: access denied (HTTP 403): Permission 'artifactregistry.packages.list' denied on resource '...' (or it may not exist).
```

That single line lands in the historical log → durable, discoverable, no spelunking. Same `jq` pattern that already exists in the file (DRY-able into a small local helper, or just inline-copy — either is fine).

## Why I'd stop there (no broader rework)

- **The historical log IS durable.** Putting actionable error content into it solves the forensic-retention problem at zero structural cost. No new directory layout, no BURV changes, no copying artifacts around.
- **The full body file remains** in BURV temp for the rare case where you want raw bytes (request/response correlation, hex dump, etc.) — within the current run, before OS cleanup.
- **Larger ergonomic improvements** (move BURV temp to project-rooted, surface artifact paths in case-level trace.txt) are real but cross several concerns and aren't load-bearing for this surprise. Capture them as itches if they recur.

So: yes, retained-but-fragile; bundle the body-summary fix with the retry-discrimination work. One tight pace, two related fixes that share the same touched function.

content.text:
You're right — that was sloppy on my part. They're different signatures with different meanings, and lumping them costs us discrimination.

## Sharpening: 403 only

**At this layer, the recognized 'not yet' signature is HTTP 403 + `IAM_PERMISSION_DENIED`.** That's the IAM-policy-read propagation race. 401 means something else entirely — "your bearer token was rejected by the auth boundary" — and we just minted that token via `rbgo_get_token_capture` which already absorbs *its* layer's race (the JWT-signature one, BBAAp). A 401 at the AR call after a successful token mint is a real auth anomaly, not propagation; absorbing it would hide actual bugs.

So the case-branch becomes:

```
case "${z_code}" in
  200|206)  ok                                         # current
  403)      if budget remaining: sleep+grow, retry     # NEW
            else:                buc_die with body     # NEW: body-summary on die
  *)        die immediately, with body summary          # current behavior, also 401 lands here
esac
```

Two consequences worth calling out:

- **401 falls into the catch-all**, which already extracts `.error.message` and surfaces it. So a 401 anomaly becomes a *fast, loud, body-included* failure — exactly what a real auth bug deserves.
- **403 within budget retries; past budget dies with body.** Same fail-fast contract, just bounded by what we know is a transient propagation race.

## The discrimination principle, restated

Each layer absorbs **only its own recognized 'not yet'**:
- L1 `rbgo_get_token_capture`: `invalid_grant + "Invalid JWT Signature."` (BBAAp). Token-mint race only.
- L2 `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once`: HTTP 403 + IAM_PERMISSION_DENIED. IAM-policy-read race only.

If a future surprise reveals a *different* signature (e.g., 401 in some lifecycle window we haven't anticipated), we treat it as a fresh observation, slate a fresh pace, and add another narrow signature to the appropriate layer. We don't pre-emptively widen.

This keeps every retry policy load-bearing — each one points at a specific, named race we've actually witnessed — and the discrimination stays sharp. Good catch on the 401.

Want me to slate the pace now with this tighter scope?

      fi
      buc_die "JWT SA probe [${z_role}] iteration ${z_iteration}: unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"
      ;;
  esac
}

### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress

Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"

# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
```

**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.

**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:

```bash
# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"

# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
local z_index=0
while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"

  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
done
```

### String Usage

```bash
# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"

# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
```

### Readonly Patterns

Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:

#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables

Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.

```bash
# ✅ Default: readonly locals
local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
```

**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:

```bash
# Exception 1: Loop counters
local z_index=0
z_index=$((z_index + 1))

# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
local z_manifest_file=""
for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  ...
done

# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

lastPrompt: That looks like sloppy coding, doesn't *) cover that case?  Also is zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once not BCG compliant?

content.text:
You're right to ask — there are real BCG violations in `zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once`. Three:

## 1. Parameter locals missing `-r` (rbgv:169–170)

```
local z_role="${1}"
local z_iteration="${2}"
```

BCG §"`local -r` — Default for Local Variables" says assigned-once locals get `-r`. Both are read-only after assignment. The helper directly above (`zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry`, line 107–112) uses `local -r` correctly for all six params — same author, same file, same function family, inconsistent application.

## 2. `2>/dev/null` on jq calls (rbgv:219–220)

```
if jq -e . "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  z_err=$(jq -r '.error.message // "Unknown error"' "${ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE}" 2>/dev/null) || ...
```

BCG §"Stderr Capture — Never Suppress" is explicit: *"Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands."* Per the rule, jq stderr should land in `${ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE}` (or a paired one) and the failure path should reference it.

## 3. The `401|403)` lump itself (rbgv:214–216)

The lump strips diagnostic richness — 403's body content (which we now know contains `Permission '...' denied on resource ... (or it may not exist).`) gets reduced to bare `(HTTP 403)`. Meanwhile the catch-all branch right below extracts `.error.message` and surfaces it. The lumped 4xx branch is doing *less* than the unknown-code branch — backwards. This is what made me spelunk to find the body in temp.

## How this rides the pace

The planned change (403 retry + body-summary on terminal die) **already touches the case statement, the locals around the parsed code, and adds budget constants**. Sweeping all three BCG violations on the same touch is cheap — same function, no extra surface:

- (1) Add `-r` to z_role and z_iteration → zero risk.
- (2) Capture jq stderr → ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE already exists; reuse it. Reference in any new die path.
- (3) Drop `401|403)`, let `*)` do its job → 401 inherits the rich diagnostic for free, 403 gets the discriminated retry, code gets simpler not denser.

This is the kind of incidental-cleanup-on-touched-surface that's load-bearing for the pace's correctness *and* doesn't become a thicket — every change is in one ~60-line function.

Want me to slate the pace now with all three sweeps in scope, or hold and surface as separate paces? My read: bundle. The retry-discrimination work *requires* touching the case branches, and any reviewer reading it will spot the same BCG nits — better to fix in the same touch than leave smell next to fresh code.

message.content: I belatedly saw that the logic you had was about retrying until a time limit only.  Would you think hard about a clean and very correct way to handle all of this? I'm not certain it was bad code after, but a deft loop adjustment (if BCG compliant!) might be correct.  Thoughts?

content.text:
You're right — the existing loop already *is* a bounded retry; what's wrong is its termination semantic. The deft adjustment is to flip what "iteration" means at the outer level, and reduce the inner to pure observation. No new budget machinery, no new helpers beyond a tiny DRY capture for jq-message extraction.

## The semantic flip

Today: `rbgv_jwt_sa_probe` loops `count` times, **every iteration must succeed**, sleeps between iterations. Stability-sampling intent.

Tomorrow: `rbgv_jwt_sa_probe` loops up to `count` times, **first 2xx wins**, retries only on 403 (the recognized 'not yet'), dies-with-body on anything else, sleeps between retries. Propagation-absorbing intent.

Same loop body. Same `count` × `delay_ms` shape. Same explicit test+increment. The discriminator is the budget — exactly what the outer loop is for.

## What moves where

**`zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once` becomes pure observation.** It does the call, populates `ZRBGV_AR_*_FILE`, and stops deciding policy. No more `case` on the HTTP code inside; no more dying on 4xx. Buc_dies only on infrastructure bugs (curl-network failure, unreadable code file). It either succeeds at *observing* or fails at *observing* — no semantic verdict.

The natural shape is to rename it to a `_capture` and have it `printf` the HTTP code to stdout. Outer captures and decides.

**`rbgv_jwt_sa_probe` holds the verdict.** Three branches, each load-bearing:

```
case "${z_code}" in
  200|206)  return 0                                        # success — first 2xx wins
  403)      if z_iter < z_count: log+sleep+continue         # 'not yet' — retry within budget
            else: buc_die "...budget exhausted: ${z_err}"   # past budget — propagation didn't resolve
  *)        buc_die "...unexpected HTTP ${z_code}: ${z_err}"# 401, 404, 4xx-other, 5xx-after-helper-exhaust
esac
```

`*)` does what we said it should: 401 lands here, 404 lands here, the catch-all gets the rich body diagnostic for free. No `401|403)` lump. No second `*)` branch. Three cases, three real semantics.

## The DRY helper that rides along

Two of the three branches need the body-summary string. Pull the existing inline jq logic into `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture(file)` — one capture function, BCG-compliant: `local -r` parameter, jq stderr captured to `ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE` (already exists, currently unused for forensic purposes), no `2>/dev/null` anywhere. Used by both die paths.

That single helper closes BCG violation #2 (stderr suppression) at the same touch. Violation #1 (`local -r` on z_role/z_iteration) closes when those locals get rewritten. Violation #3 (`401|403)` lump) closes by construction — the lump simply doesn't exist in the new shape.

## Call-site tuning, not branching

The fixture invocations of `tt/rbtd-ap.AccessProbe.{role}.sh` carry `count=5 delay_ms=1500` — sized for stability sampling (~7.5s). Under the new semantic those become "max attempts × delay = budget", and IAM propagation wants ~60–120s. So call sites adjust to e.g. `count=40 delay_ms=3000`. Pure config; no branching.

## What's lost — and why I think it's not load-bearing

Stability sampling. Once GCP's AR endpoint returns 2xx to a JWT-bearer'd request in a region, the binding is durable from that client; flap-after-success isn't a real mode in the lifecycle this probe inhabits. If a future surprise reveals it is, we add a stable-confirm wrapper then — at that point the requirement names itself. Today, removing it sheds non-load-bearing scaffolding.

## Why this is "clean and very correct"

- **Inner does one thing** (observe), **outer does one thing** (decide), **policy lives where it has loop context** (outer). Each function earns its existence.
- **No new files, no new constants, no new branches.** One semantic flip on the existing loop, one small DRY helper, one rename.
- **Failure modes are sharply distinguished** — propagation timeout reads "budget exhausted: <body>", real anomaly reads "unexpected HTTP 401: <body>", infrastructure bug reads "curl failed/empty code". Operator triages by reading.
- **BCG sweep rides for free** — the changes mechanically reach the violations.
- **Stack composes with BBAAp** — token mint absorbs its race at L1, this probe absorbs its race at L2, neither layer knows about the other.

Want me to slate the pace with this scope: rename probe_once → probe_once_capture, semantic flip in rbgv_jwt_sa_probe, new zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture, drop `401|403)` lump, fix the two BCG nits, tune call-site defaults?

24	test -z "${ZRBGV_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgv multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
25	ZRBGV_SOURCED=1
26	
27	######################################################################
28	# Internal Functions (zrbgv_*)
29	
30	zrbgv_kindle() {
31	  test -z "${ZRBGV_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgv already kindled"
32	
33	  buc_log_args "Ensure dependencies are kindled first"
34	  zrbgc_sentinel
35	  zrbgo_sentinel
36	  zrbgu_sentinel
37	  zrbgp_sentinel
38	
39	  # Kindle-constant temp file paths for forensic visibility
40	  readonly ZRBGV_AR_RESP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_resp.json"
41	  readonly ZRBGV_AR_CODE_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_code.txt"
42	  readonly ZRBGV_AR_STDERR_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_ar_stderr.txt"
43	  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_RESP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_resp.json"
44	  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_CODE_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_code.txt"
45	  readonly ZRBGV_CRM_STDERR_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgv_crm_stderr.txt"
46	
47	  # Transient-5xx retry policy: 3 attempts with initial 2s backoff doubling each retry (worst-case +6s)
48	  readonly ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS=3
49	  readonly ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC=2
50	
51	  readonly ZRBGV_KINDLED=1
52	}
53	
54	zrbgv_sentinel() {
55	  test "${ZRBGV_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbgv not kindled - call zrbgv_kindle first"
56	}
57	
58	# Convert milliseconds to a decimal seconds string suitable for sleep
59	zrbgv_ms_to_sleep_capture() {
60	  zrbgv_sentinel
61	
62	  local z_ms="${1}"
63	
64	  buc_log_args "Converting ${z_ms}ms to sleep duration"
65	
66	  # Compute whole seconds and remainder milliseconds using integer arithmetic
67	  local z_sec=$(( z_ms / 1000 ))
68	  local z_rem=$(( z_ms % 1000 ))
69	
70	  # Format as decimal with three fractional digits (sleep accepts e.g. 1.500)
71	  printf '%d.%03d' "${z_sec}" "${z_rem}"
72	}
73	
74	# Resolve the RBRA file path for a given role name
75	# Role names: governor | director | retriever
76	zrbgv_role_rbra_file_capture() {
77	  zrbgv_sentinel
78	
79	  local z_role="${1}"
80	
81	  case "${z_role}" in
82	    governor)
83	      test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}"  || buc_die "RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE is not set"
84	      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}"
85	      ;;
86	    director)
87	      test -n "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE:-}"  || buc_die "RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE is not set"
88	      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}"
89	      ;;
90	    retriever)
91	      test -n "${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE:-}" || buc_die "RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE is not set"
92	      printf '%s\n' "${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE}"
93	      ;;
94	    *)
95	      buc_die "Unknown role '${z_role}': expected governor | director | retriever"
96	      ;;

98	}
99	
100	# HTTP GET with bounded exponential-backoff retry on transient 5xx responses.
101	# On any non-5xx response (including auth errors), populates z_code_file and returns.
102	# On curl-network failure or exhausted 5xx retries, buc_die. The caller evaluates
103	# the final HTTP status via its own case-switch on the populated z_code_file.
104	zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry() {
105	  zrbgv_sentinel
106	
107	  local -r z_label="${1}"
108	  local -r z_url="${2}"
109	  local -r z_token="${3}"
110	  local -r z_resp_file="${4}"
111	  local -r z_code_file="${5}"
112	  local -r z_stderr_file="${6}"
113	
114	  local z_attempt=1
115	  local z_delay="${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC}"
116	  local z_curl_status=0
117	  local z_code=""
118	
119	  while test "${z_attempt}" -le "${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS}"; do
120	    buc_log_args "${z_label}: HTTP GET attempt ${z_attempt}/${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS}"
121	
122	    z_curl_status=0
123	    curl                                                     \
124	        -sS                                                  \
125	        -X GET                                               \
126	        -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}"                \
127	        -H "Accept: application/json"                        \
128	        -o "${z_resp_file}"                                  \
129	        -w "%{http_code}"                                    \
130	        --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
131	        --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}"               \
132	        "${z_url}" > "${z_code_file}"                        \
133	                   2> "${z_stderr_file}"                     \
134	      || z_curl_status=$?
135	
136	    buc_log_args "${z_label}: curl exit status ${z_curl_status}"
137	    buc_log_pipe < "${z_stderr_file}"
138	
139	    test "${z_curl_status}" -eq 0 \
140	      || buc_die "${z_label}: curl failed (network/SSL/DNS) — see ${z_stderr_file}"
141	
142	    z_code=$(<"${z_code_file}") || buc_die "${z_label}: failed to read HTTP code file"
143	    test -n "${z_code}" || buc_die "${z_label}: empty HTTP code from curl"
144	
145	    case "${z_code}" in
146	      500|502|503|504)
147	        if test "${z_attempt}" -lt "${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS}"; then
148	          buc_step "${z_label}: transient HTTP ${z_code}, retrying in ${z_delay}s (attempt ${z_attempt}/${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS})"
149	          sleep "${z_delay}"
150	          z_delay=$(( z_delay * 2 ))
151	          z_attempt=$(( z_attempt + 1 ))
152	        else
153	          buc_die "${z_label}: repeated transient HTTP ${z_code} after ${ZRBGV_HTTP_RETRY_ATTEMPTS} attempts — see ${z_resp_file}"
154	        fi
155	        ;;
156	      *)
157	        return 0
158	        ;;
159	    esac
160	  done
161	}
162	
163	# Execute one Artifact Registry packages.list probe iteration for a JWT SA role.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");

# You may obtain a copy of the License at
